args.code:
import re
import pandas as pd
import json

data = locals()['var_function-call-17451983045047543409']
# data is a list of dicts

parsed_data = []
for entry in data:
    bid = entry['business_id']
    rc = int(entry['review_count'])
    desc = entry['description']
    
    # Extract state
    # Look for ", XX, this"
    match = re.search(r",\s([A-Z]{2}),\sthis", desc)
    if match:
        state = match.group(1)
        parsed_data.append({'business_id': bid, 'state': state, 'review_count': rc})
    else:
        # Fallback or print error to debug
        # Maybe " in City, XX" at end of string if no ", this"
        # But all examples have ", this"
        pass

df = pd.DataFrame(parsed_data)

# Group by state and sum review_count
state_counts = df.groupby('state')['review_count'].sum().reset_index()
state_counts = state_counts.sort_values(by='review_count', ascending=False)

top_state = state_counts.iloc[0]['state']
top_state_reviews = state_counts.iloc[0]['review_count']

# Get business_ids for top state
top_state_businesses = df[df['state'] == top_state]['business_id'].tolist()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    "top_state": top_state,
    "total_reviews": int(top_state_reviews),
    "business_ids": top_state_businesses
}))

var_function-call-9282625743614851059.name: Steps to Learning Montessori Preschool
var_function-call-9282625743614851059.description: Located at 6901 Phelps Rd in Goleta, CA, this facility offers a nurturing environment for young learners, providing a range of services in Education, Elementary Schools, Child Care & Day Care, Local Services, Preschools, and Montessori Schools.

var_function-call-1367985186306475616.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

file_path = locals()['var_function-call-1471230721196945847']

with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

parsed_data = []
for entry in data:
    bid = entry['business_id']
    try:
        rc = int(entry['review_count'])
    except ValueError:
        rc = 0
    desc = entry['description']
    
    # Regex to find state
    # Pattern 1: ", [State], this"
    # Pattern 2: " [State] location"
    # Pattern 3: ", [State] location"
    
    # Combined regex: Look for 2 uppercase letters followed by ", this" or " location"
    # Also handle the comma before the state if present, but the state is the group.
    
    match = re.search(r"[\s,]([A-Z]{2})(?:,\sthis|\slocation)", desc)
    
    if match:
        state = match.group(1)
        parsed_data.append({'business_id': bid, 'state': state, 'review_count': rc})
    else:
        # Try a fallback for "Located in [City], [State], this" where there is no address
        # It should be covered by ", [State], this" pattern since ", this" is the anchor.
        # "Located in Pennsauken, NJ, this" -> "NJ" matches ", NJ, this" part?
        # Wait, "NJ, this" matches `,\sthis`? No, the comma is before `this`.
        # "NJ, this" -> comma is after NJ.
        # "St. Louis, MO, this" -> comma after MO.
        # So pattern is `([A-Z]{2}),\sthis`.
        
        # "Philadelphia, PA location" -> space after PA.
        
        match2 = re.search(r"([A-Z]{2}),\sthis", desc)
        match3 = re.search(r"([A-Z]{2})\slocation", desc)
        
        if match2:
            state = match2.group(1)
            parsed_data.append({'business_id': bid, 'state': state, 'review_count': rc})
        elif match3:
            state = match3.group(1)
            parsed_data.append({'business_id': bid, 'state': state, 'review_count': rc})
        else:
            # print(f"Could not parse state for: {desc}")
            pass

df = pd.DataFrame(parsed_data)

# Group by state
state_counts = df.groupby('state')['review_count'].sum().reset_index()
state_counts = state_counts.sort_values(by='review_count', ascending=False)

if not state_counts.empty:
    top_state = state_counts.iloc[0]['state']
    top_state_reviews = state_counts.iloc[0]['review_count']
    
    top_businesses = df[df['state'] == top_state]['business_id'].tolist()
    
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(json.dumps({
        "top_state": top_state,
        "total_reviews": int(top_state_reviews),
        "business_ids": top_businesses
    }))
else:
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(json.dumps({"error": "No data found"}))

var_function-call-9282625743614851059.name: Steps to Learning Montessori Preschool
var_function-call-9282625743614851059.description: Located at 6901 Phelps Rd in Goleta, CA, this facility offers a nurturing environment for young learners, providing a range of services in Education, Elementary Schools, Child Care & Day Care, Local Services, Preschools, and Montessori Schools.

var_function-call-1367985186306475616.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

# data is a dict string? No, result of execute_python is printed, but the variable stores the printed string?

print(json.dumps({"sql_list": sql_list}))

var_function-call-1367985186306475616.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

args.query: SELECT business_ref, AVG(rating) as avg_rating FROM review WHERE business_ref IN ('businessref_47', 'businessref_64', 'businessref_10', 'businessref_24', 'businessref_30', 'businessref_96', 'businessref_11', 'businessref_4', 'businessref_46') GROUP BY business_ref

exec_args.sql: SELECT business_ref, AVG(rating) as avg_rating FROM review WHERE business_ref IN ('businessref_47', 'businessref_64', 'businessref_10', 'businessref_24', 'businessref_30', 'businessref_96', 'businessref_11', 'businessref_4', 'businessref_46') GROUP BY business_ref

args.code:
import json

data = locals()['var_function-call-7487128546928655759']
# data is list of dicts

ratings = [float(entry['avg_rating']) for entry in data]
avg_of_businesses = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"state": "MO", "average_rating": avg_of_businesses}))
